figures.caption: Table 4: Comparison of test time scaling methods across benchmarks and models. We report accuracy (Acc, ↑ better) and token usage (#Tokens, ↓ better) across four benchmarks. Results include mean and standard deviation over 5 random seeds for AMC23 vs AIME24 and 3 random seeds for Minerva vs MATH500. Bold indicates best performance; underline indicates runner-up.

Figure 3: Runtime comparison across test-time scaling methods on MATH500. Our proposed methods (PB-SMC and SPS) scale linearly in the number of tokens, and carry only a small runtime overhead of roughly 10% in comparison with the nearest competitive baselines (beam search, DVTS). Taking into account the token count advantage of our methods (3X), this overhead becomes negligible.

Table 2: Comparison of test time scaling methods across benchmarks and models. We report accuracy (Acc, ↑ better) and token usage (#Tokens, ↓ better) across four benchmarks. Results include mean and standard deviation over 5 random seeds for AMC23 vs AIME24 and 3 random seeds for Minerva vs MATH500. Bold indicates best performance; underline indicates runner-up.

Figure 2: Accuracy–compute trade-off on MATH500 using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Each curve varies the number of parallel trajectories from N = 4 to N = 64. Panel (a) reports accuracy versus generated tokens, while panel (b) reports accuracy versus wall-clock runtime. PB-SMC and SPS improve the Pareto front under both compute measures, achieving higher accuracy at a fixed budget or requiring less compute for a target accuracy.

Figure 1: Schematic comparison of frontier-only search and persistent-pool backtracking. Frontieronly methods keep only the newly generated children Bt, so prefixes not selected into the next frontier cannot be revisited. Greedy Selection keeps a persistent pool and applies global top ranking, but older correct prefixes are blocked by (always scored lower than) prefixes already selected at t− 1. SPS resolves this by ranking only inside a random subpool, giving blocked historical prefixes another chance to be expanded. Power Backtrack SMC keeps historical prefixes through weighted resampling, combining a retained historical multiset St with newly generated children Bt.

Table 3: Ablation of historical backtracking and stochastic persistent-pool access with Qwen2.57B-Instruct. We report accuracy (Acc, %, ↑ better), and average token usage (#Tokens, ↓ better). Bold indicates best accuracy; underline indicates runner-up.

Table 1: Instantiation of Algorithm 1. Beam search and standard SMC are frontier-only because their next pool contains only Bt. Greedy Selection, SPS, and Power Backtrack SMC keep historical prefixes selectable, either by multiset union or by resampling historical particles.
